titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Sheila Levrant de Bretteville Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Adrienne Pruitt

publicationstmt.publisher: Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday December 12, 2025 at 19:01

corpname.part: Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library Special Collections

descriptivenote.p: The majority of the materials are in English, with a small amount of material in Italian.

did.abstract: This collection includes examples of Sheila Levrant de Bretteville's graphic design work and public artworks from all eras of her career, as well as a small amount of biographical materials.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection is arranged roughly chronologically, and then by project or institution where practicable.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Sheila Levrant de Bretteville (born 1940) is a public artist, graphic designer, and educator known for her focus on inclusive, participatory design and feminist principles. She served as director of graduate studies in graphic design at Yale from 1990-2022 and became the first woman at the Yale School of Art to be awarded tenure.

Sheila Levrant de Bretteville was born on November 4, 1940, in Brooklyn, NY, to Hellen (Gruenwald) and Israel Levrant. Immigrants from Poland, her parents worked in the textile and millinery industries. De Bretteville was exposed to art through classes at the Brooklyn Museum and at Abraham Lincoln High School, where she was taught by Leon Friend. Friend's book Graphic Design (1936) impressed her with its notion of graphic design as an inclusive endeavor. As part of Friend's extracurricular "Art Squad," de Bretteville won the Alex Steinweiss prize, among others, for her work.

De Bretteville received her BA in art history from Barnard in 1962 and her MFA in graphic design from Yale in 1964. After working for Yale University Press and Chanticleer Press, she took a job at Olivetti in Milan, Italy, returning to New York in the fall of 1969. She was offered work doing the branding for the newly established California Institute of the Arts (known as CalArts) in Los Angeles and became the first female faculty in the design department there in 1970. In 1971 she created the first women's design program at CalArts. Two years later she would leave to co-found the Woman's Building in Los Angeles along with Judy Chicago and Arlene Raven. The Woman's Building housed the Feminist Studio Workshop, an independent art school for women, and the Women's Graphic Center, a design and printing operation. De Bretteville was hired to create and chair a new Communication Arts Department at the Otis College of Art and Design in 1980, and remained on the board of the Woman's Building until the closure of the Feminist Studio Workshop in 1981.

In 1990 de Bretteville returned to Yale as the director of graduate studies in graphic design, looking to bring "democratic pluralism and activism and a focus on what's been left out" to the program. Paul Rand, professor of graphic design at Yale since 1956 and noted for his emphasis on formal modernism, resigned in 1992 in protest, convincing his longtime friend Armin Hofmann to do the same. In 2010 she was named the Caroline M. Street Professor of Graphic Design, and became Professor Emeritus in 2025.

De Bretteville's interests in community and democratic spaces that invite individual participation and interpretation can be seen in her public artworks. Her site-specific public artworks have included Path of Stars (1993) and Hillhouse (2003) in New Haven, Connecticut; Biddy Mason: Time and Place (1989-1990) and Omoide no Shotokyo (1994) in Los Angeles; West End Echoes (1995) and Hear Us (1999) in Boston; At the Start . . . At Long Last in the 207th Street MTA station in New York City (1999); and Step(pe) in Yekaterinburg, Russa (2006).

Her design work has included the redesign of the Los Angeles Times, special issues of publications including Everywoman, The Aspen Times, American Cinematographer, Arts in Society, and design work on numerous books including The Motown Album. De Bretteville holds honorary degrees from the Maryland Institute College of Art, California College of the Arts, Moore College of Art, and Otis College of Art and Design. She received the American Institute of Graphic Arts (AIGA) medal in 2004 and was declared a Design Legend by the AIGA in 2006.
De Bretteville married her husband, Peter, in 1965, and had one child, Jason, in 1970.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: The majority of the materials in this collection were originally on display at the Yale University Art Gallery, in "Sheila Levrant de Bretteville: Community, Activism, and Design" February 16, 2024 – June 23, 2024, and were transferred to ALSC in February 2025. The collection also includes materials gathered for that exhibition but not displayed (also transferred by the Gallery) and a small number of materials that had been acquired previously. The separate provenance of these three items is noted at the item-level.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: This collection includes examples of graphic designer, artist, and educator Sheila Levrant de Bretteville's graphic design and public artworks, as well as a small amount of biographical materials. Items range from student work at Yale and commercial projects done for Olivetti in Italy, to materials produced at the California Institute of the Arts and the Woman's Building in Los Angeles, to documentation of public art installations done worldwide. Formats include posters, prints, brochures, invitations and announcements, stationery, mock-ups, photographs, bound volumes, and project sketches and schematics. There is also realia including decals, stickers, tiles, t-shirts, and a scale model of "Biddy Mason: Time and Place." The collection includes little correspondence. The collection also lacks any teaching materials or administrative records from her time as a professor and program director.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright is retained by Sheila Levrant de Bretteville for materials she has authored or otherwise produced. After the lifetime of Sheila Levrant de Bretteville, copyright passes to Yale University. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user. Please include appropriate citation to the artist if reproducing or otherwise sharing materials in the collection.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Sheila Levrant de Bretteville, 2024.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Sheila Levrant de Bretteville papers, Robert B. Haas Family Arts Library, Yale University.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Related material: School of Art, Yale University, records (RU 291)

did.unittitle: "International Design Conference in Aspen: Paradox" registration card
"International Design Conference in Aspen 1971" laminated comment card
"International Design Conference in Aspen 1971" participant kit
The Feminist Studio Workshop, The Women's Graphic Center, and The Woman's Building

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The Feminist Studio Workshop, the Women's Graphic Center, and the Woman's Building led intertwined existences. In 1973, Sheila Levrant de Bretteville left the California Institute of the Arts to co-found the Feminist Studio Workshop with art historian Arlene Raven. The Feminist Studio Workshop was an independent art school for women. After initially meeting in de Bretteville's living room, the Workshop found a home in the Woman's Building, co-founded by de Bretteville, Raven, and Judy Chicago.

The Woman's Building was named for Chicago's 1893 World's Columbian Exposition building, designed by a female architect to house women's artworks. In fall 1973, the new Woman's Building was established in the former Chouinard Art Institute building at 743 South Grandview Boulevard in Los Angeles, California. The building housed a variety of women's organizations, including the magazine Chrysalis and the Women's Graphic Center, a design and printing operation. Although it changed location in 1975, the Woman's Building continued to host art, education, printing, and performance before closing in 1991.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Framed, as received from Sheila Levrant de Bretteville.

scopecontent.p: Silver-colored neck chain necklace with 5 cm standard commercial eye bolt pendant representing the gender symbol.

acqinfo.p: Gift of Sheila Levrant de Bretteville, 2021. Exhibited in "Lux et Femina: Women in Graphic Design at Yale" at the Haas Family Arts Library, 2021-2022.

did.unittitle: "Women In Design: The Next Decade" poster

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poster for the "Women in design: the next decade, a conference for women who work with public visual and physical forms, March 20th, [1975] at the Woman's Building, 1727 North Spring Street, Los Angeles, California 90012." Images of the gender symbol for women shown as eye bolt in beige on a mauve background, some of which is displayed as a grid.
